ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore economic rice mixed plate; mapped to closest parent class 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_00002490) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singapore economic rice; A00LC (rice-based mixed dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific mixed rice dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with one vegetable dish and one fried meat dish typically weighs approximately 350 g as served at a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 1 Fried Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 1 Fried Meat), cr_id F05100031. All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.8 g/100g based on HPB compositional data for comparable fried-meat rice dishes in the same subgroup.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 applied based on University of Sydney GI Database value for white rice (jasmine/steamed), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 66.5) / 100 ≈ 47.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.99 × 3.5 = 66.5 g. The fried meat and vegetable components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. Input record GI confidence of 0.476 noted; GI value of 72 is consistent with University of Sydney white rice reference and is retained.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 236 mg/100g with low potassium and fibre offset; saturated fat from fried meat contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, request reduced soy sauce or seasoning, and remove the fried meat coating or skin to lower saturated fat; sodium at 236 mg/100g is borderline and accumulates significantly at a full 350 g serving (~826 mg sodium per plate).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) with low dietary fibre (0.95 g/100g) drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation; deep-fried meat adds fat that worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high glycemic index of 72 and low fibre content; if consumed, request a smaller rice portion, substitute brown rice where available, and choose steamed or grilled meat instead of fried to reduce the combined glycaemic and metabolic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried meat preparation increases saturated fat and trans fat exposure; sodium load at 236 mg/100g contributes to blood pressure elevation; refined white rice raises triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to the combined effect of deep-fried meat (elevated saturated fat and potential trans fat from repeated frying oil use), borderline sodium content, and refined carbohydrate base that raises triglycerides; substituting with steamed or grilled protein and requesting less sauce significantly reduces cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried meat generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; refined white rice contributes glycaemic-load-driven systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried meat preparations as AGEs worsen joint inflammation; request steamed, grilled, or braised meat alternatives and consider brown rice to reduce the overall pro-inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 1 Fried Meat), known in Mandarin as 经济饭 (jīngjì fàn) and in Malay as Nasi Ekonomi, is one of Singapore's most ubiquitous and affordable hawker-centre meals. The dish consists of a base of steamed white jasmine rice served alongside one portion of a stir-fried or blanched vegetable dish and one portion of deep-fried meat — commonly pork cutlet, fried chicken, or battered fish. The rice is soft and slightly sticky, the vegetable dish is typically seasoned with garlic and oyster sauce, and the fried meat provides a crispy, savoury contrast. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 160 kcal, 5.71 g protein, 6.51 g fat, and 18.99 g carbohydrate, with 0.95 g dietary fibre and 236 mg sodium. The high-GI white rice base (GI 72) combined with low fibre makes this dish a moderate-to-high glycaemic concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The deep-fried meat component elevates saturated fat intake and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Sodium at 236 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension, accumulating to approximately 826 mg per standard 350 g plate. This dish is a staple working-lunch option across Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops (kopitiams), consumed daily by a broad demographic. Healthier modifications include requesting brown rice, choosing steamed or grilled protein, and reducing sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic rice with 1 veg and 1 fried meat?
Is nasi ekonomi suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100031) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI value of 72 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database reference for white rice. Saturated fat estimated from comparable HPB entries in the same subgroup.
notes: Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from HPB compositional data for comparable fried-meat rice dishes in the same subgroup, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available from other approved sources for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as this dish contains fried meat and is not vegetarian or plant-based. Halal status set to true as economic rice stalls in Singapore are predominantly halal-certified; this should be verified at point of purchase. GI glycemic load per serving calculated at 350 g standard plate size.
